summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the claim that UI design is still in its infancy, pointing to documented UI pattern libraries and impressive demos from Windows Vista and Office 12 at PDC 2005. He acknowledges there's still progress to be made but argues the field has matured significantly. The post highlights Microsoft's strategy of incorporating web metaphors—like back/forward buttons, single-task pages, and ubiquitous search—into desktop applications. Atwood sees this convergence of web and desktop UI patterns as a positive evolution in interface design.
key_points: UI design has matured beyond the 'stone age' as evidenced by documented pattern libraries
Microsoft is aggressively incorporating web metaphors into Vista and Office
Web-native concepts like forward/back buttons, single-task pages, and search boxes are becoming desktop standards
The existence of formal UI pattern documentation suggests the field has reached a level of professional maturity
money_quotes: If UI design is in the stone age, why are there at least two sites which document known UI patterns?

I was struck by how aggressively Microsoft has folded web metaphors into Vista and Office – things like the forward and back buttons, focusing on a single task per page, and the ubiquitous search box.
key_insight: The cross-pollination of web interface conventions into desktop software signals that UI design has evolved past its primitive era into a more mature discipline with documented patterns and shared metaphors.
